skill.name: mcp-builder-ms
skill.description: Guides developers through planning, building, testing, and evaluating MCP servers for Microsoft, TypeScript, Python, and C# ecosystems.
skill.summary: Build well-designed MCP servers with practical workflow, SDK, testing, and evaluation guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found this is a Markdown guide rather than executable code. The weak-cryptography and hidden-file findings are false positives; the meaningful risks are documented network fetches and example commands that users may run during MCP development.

medium_findings.title: External Command Guidance Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The guide tells users to run npm, npx, and Python compile commands while testing MCP servers. This is legitimate development guidance, but npm scripts and local project files can execute arbitrary code if the target project is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in the Markdown guide and are relevant to MCP testing. Risk depends on the surrounding project, so this is a real but contextual concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Network Fetch Guidance Is Expected But Material
medium_findings.description: The guide instructs users to fetch public MCP and SDK documentation from modelcontextprotocol.io and raw.githubusercontent.com. These are expected references for this skill, but the skill should be used with awareness that it directs network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced URLs are visible in the guide and align with the stated MCP development purpose. I did not find evidence that sensitive data is sent to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm indicators across prose, headings, and Markdown examples. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code, hash functions, encryption APIs, or instructions to use weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is a documentation guide and the flagged lines do not implement cryptography. The repeated hits appear to come from token matches in prose and Markdown formatting.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The hidden-file findings point to C#/.NET and Microsoft MCP references, not reads or writes to dotfiles. I did not find instructions to access hidden files or hidden directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged location is documentation text about .NET or Microsoft MCP resources. There is no file access operation at those lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Example Shell Commands In Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples include commands for building and testing MCP servers. They are not executed by the skill itself, but users should inspect project scripts before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are present and actionable. They are normal development commands, so the risk is contextual.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Public Documentation URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes public URLs for MCP specifications and SDK documentation. These support the skill purpose and do not show exfiltration behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly listed as documentation sources. They are external network targets, but their use is transparent and purpose-aligned.

content.value_statement: MCP server development can become inconsistent when teams mix protocol design, SDK setup, tool schemas, and evaluation work. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for planning, implementing, testing, and evaluating MCP servers.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Microsoft MCP servers versus a custom MCP server.
Guides research using MCP protocol documentation and SDK documentation.
Covers TypeScript, Python, and C# implementation planning at a high level.
Recommends tool naming, schemas, annotations, pagination, and error handling practices.
Provides build, inspector, syntax-check, and evaluation workflow guidance.
limitations: It is a guide, not a complete MCP server template or generator.
It references language-specific guides that are not included in this single file.
It expects users to verify current SDK documentation before implementation.
It does not provide production authentication code or deployment automation.

use_cases.title: Plan a Custom MCP Integration
use_cases.description: Map an external API into MCP tools with clear naming, schemas, pagination, and error handling before writing code.

use_cases.title: Choose Microsoft or Custom MCP
use_cases.description: Decide whether Azure MCP, Foundry MCP, Fabric MCP, or a custom server best fits a Microsoft-focused integration.

use_cases.title: Prepare MCP Quality Checks
use_cases.description: Create a testing and evaluation plan that checks build quality, inspector behavior, and realistic read-only questions.

prompt_templates.title: Begin an MCP Server Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder-ms skill to help me plan an MCP server for this API. Identify the core tools, transport choice, authentication needs, and first implementation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder-ms skill to design tool names, input schemas, output structure, annotations, pagination, and error messages for these API operations.

prompt_templates.title: Review an MCP Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder-ms skill to review my MCP server design. Check protocol fit, tool discoverability, structured output, safety annotations, and testing coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Create MCP Evaluations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder-ms skill to create an evaluation plan with ten realistic read-only questions, expected answers, and verification guidance for this MCP server.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a customer support ticket API.
output_examples.output: A phased plan covering API research, tool selection, schema design, pagination, authentication, build checks, inspector testing, and evaluation questions.

output_examples.input: Review whether to use Azure MCP or a custom server.
output_examples.output: A recommendation comparing existing Microsoft MCP coverage, custom workflow needs, authentication model, deployment target, and maintenance cost.

output_examples.input: Create evaluation ideas for an MCP server.
output_examples.output: A set of realistic read-only questions with stable expected answers and notes on how to verify each answer.

best_practices: Start with protocol and SDK documentation before choosing a framework or transport.
Use clear action-oriented tool names, structured outputs, and accurate safety annotations.
Test with build checks, MCP Inspector, and realistic read-only evaluation questions.
anti_patterns: Do not expose every API endpoint without designing useful agent workflows.
Do not run project commands from untrusted repositories without inspecting scripts first.
Do not create evaluations that depend on mutable data or destructive operations.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It guides planning, implementation choices, testing, and evaluation. Users still write the server code.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It discusses TypeScript, Python, and C#/.NET paths for MCP server development.

faq.question: Is it specific to Microsoft services?
faq.answer: It includes Microsoft MCP guidance, but it also applies to custom third-party and internal API integrations.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: It recommends fetching public MCP and SDK documentation, so network access may be used during research.

faq.question: Is this safe to use with untrusted projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. The guide suggests running build and test commands, which can execute scripts in the target project.

faq.question: What makes a good MCP evaluation?
faq.answer: A good evaluation is read-only, realistic, independent, stable over time, and has a verifiable expected answer.
